name: Python & Scripting
description: Python for DevOps and automation: syntax, environments, files, subprocess, and the idioms that keep scripts honest.

questions.text: A Python list differs from a tuple in that a list…
options: can hold only items of one type
is always sorted automatically
is mutable — you can add, remove and change items
cannot be looped over with an ordinary for statement
questions.explanation: Lists are mutable, tuples are immutable — that's the core distinction; both are ordered and iterable.

questions.text: A dictionary (dict) stores…
options: key-value pairs with fast lookup by key
a sorted sequence of guaranteed-unique numbers
lines of text read from a file
a fixed-size table of columns
questions.explanation: Dicts map keys to values — the workhorse structure for configs, JSON data and lookups.

questions.text: A virtual environment (venv) exists to…
options: run Python code inside a lightweight dedicated virtual machine
compile Python to native machine code
sandbox scripts away from the network
isolate a project's dependencies from the system Python
questions.explanation: Each venv has its own site-packages, so projects can pin different library versions without fighting each other or the OS.

questions.text: An f-string like f"host={host}" is…
options: a string that is read from a file handle
string interpolation — {host} is replaced by the variable's value
a bytes literal for binary protocols
a strict string that raises whenever host is undefined at import time
questions.explanation: f-strings evaluate the expressions in braces at runtime — the modern, readable way to build strings.

questions.text: The idiomatic way to read a file safely is…
options: with open(path) as f: — the file closes automatically, even on errors
open(path) followed by carefully remembering to call close() at the end
os.read(path) which handles closing internally
file(path).readall() inside a try block
questions.explanation: The with statement (context manager) guarantees the file is closed however the block exits — no leaked handles.

questions.text: try/except in Python is used to…
options: run two alternative branches of code in parallel
skip code that has syntax errors
handle runtime errors instead of crashing
test code before committing it
questions.explanation: Exceptions propagate up until caught; try/except lets you handle expected failures (missing file, bad input) gracefully.

questions.text: pip is…
options: Python's built-in code formatter
the command-line interpreter that actually executes .py files
a linter that flags unused imports
Python's package installer, pulling libraries from PyPI
questions.explanation: pip installs packages (and their dependencies) from the Python Package Index into the active environment.

questions.text: range(3) produces…
options: three random integers
questions.explanation: range starts at 0 and stops before the end value — so range(3) yields 0, 1, 2.

questions.text: To turn a JSON string into Python data you use…
options: json.dumps(text)
json.loads(text)
json.open(text)
json.parse(text)
questions.explanation: loads = load from string, load = from file; dumps/dump go the other way. (json.parse is JavaScript!)

questions.text: The shebang line #!/usr/bin/env python3 at the top of a script…
options: imports the env module before anything else
pins the script permanently to one exact installed Python version
lets the OS run the file directly with the python3 found on PATH
is a comment with no effect of any kind
questions.explanation: With the executable bit set, the kernel uses the shebang to pick the interpreter — env finds python3 wherever it lives on PATH.

questions.text: A list comprehension like [x*2 for x in nums if x > 0]…
options: modifies nums in place for efficiency
is only valid inside a function body
runs the loop lazily, producing one item at a time only on demand
builds a new list by transforming and filtering in one expression
questions.explanation: Comprehensions build new lists declaratively; the lazy one-at-a-time variant is a generator expression with round brackets.

questions.text: def add(item, bucket=[]) is a classic bug because…
options: the default list is created once and shared across every call
lists are not allowed as default arguments
bucket becomes a global variable automatically
Python deep-copies the entire list on every call, wasting memory
questions.explanation: Defaults are evaluated once at function definition — the same list persists between calls. Use bucket=None and create it inside.

questions.text: The recommended way to run an external command and capture its output is…
options: os.system(cmd), which conveniently returns the output as a string
subprocess.run([...], capture_output=True, check=True)
eval(cmd) with the command in backticks
exec(cmd) inside a try block
questions.explanation: subprocess.run with a list of args avoids shell injection, captures stdout/stderr, and check=True raises on non-zero exit.

questions.text: The if __name__ == "__main__": guard exists so that…
options: the script refuses to run without root privileges
Python knows which function to call first
the block runs when the file is executed directly, but not when imported
the whole module is fully compiled down to bytecode before ever being executed
questions.explanation: When imported, __name__ is the module's name, not "__main__" — so the guard keeps script behaviour out of importers.

questions.text: requirements.txt with pinned versions (pkg==1.2.3) gives you…
options: automatic security patching for every one of your dependencies
smaller installed package sizes
faster imports at runtime
reproducible installs — the same versions everywhere
questions.explanation: Pinning makes CI, teammates and production install identical versions — the same reason the Node world uses lockfiles with npm ci.

questions.text: enumerate(items) is the idiomatic way to…
options: get both the index and the value in a for loop
count exactly how many items match a given condition
sort items while preserving duplicates
convert any iterable into a dictionary
questions.explanation: for i, item in enumerate(items): beats manually maintaining a counter — clearer and less error-prone.

questions.text: pathlib.Path is generally preferred over string paths because it…
options: loads file contents into memory noticeably faster than open() with strings
gives portable path joins and rich operations like .exists() and .glob()
encrypts paths against tampering
works only on POSIX, avoiding Windows quirks
questions.explanation: Path('/data') / 'state.json' handles separators per-OS and bundles file operations on the object itself.

questions.text: except Exception: pass scattered through a script is dangerous because it…
options: slows the interpreter down measurably
only works inside functions, not at top level
silently swallows every error, hiding real failures
catches even syntax errors and quietly corrupts the bytecode
questions.explanation: Blanket-and-ignore hides bugs and partial failures; catch the narrowest exception you can handle and at least log the rest.

questions.text: argparse is the standard-library way to…
options: parse YAML and INI configuration files loaded from disk at startup
validate JSON request bodies
split a string on whitespace
define command-line flags with help text and validation
questions.explanation: argparse declares options and arguments, generates --help, converts types and errors out cleanly on bad input.

questions.text: Using the requests library, resp.raise_for_status() is useful because…
options: it raises an exception on 4xx/5xx instead of failing silently later
it retries the request until it succeeds
it validates the server's TLS certificate chain manually on each call
it converts the body to JSON automatically
questions.explanation: requests does not raise on HTTP error codes by default — a 500 quietly gives you resp anyway unless you check.

questions.text: The GIL (Global Interpreter Lock) in CPython means…
options: imports are locked while any thread runs
only one thread executes Python bytecode at a time, limiting CPU-bound threading
garbage collection pauses all I/O operations
each Python process is permanently limited to a single CPU core for its whole lifetime
questions.explanation: Threads still help for I/O-bound work (waiting on network/disk), but CPU-bound parallelism needs multiprocessing or native extensions.

questions.text: A generator function (using yield)…
options: runs entirely on first call and caches all results
spawns a background thread per iteration
produces values lazily, keeping only its current state in memory
can only ever be consumed a single time by exactly one iterating for loop
questions.explanation: Generators compute each value on demand — ideal for streaming large files or infinite sequences without loading everything.

questions.text: Type hints like def fetch(url: str) -> dict: are…
options: enforced at runtime, raising on a wrong type
strictly required for the code to compile under Python 3.12 and later
comments that the interpreter strips at parse time
static annotations checked by tools like mypy, ignored at runtime
questions.explanation: Python stays dynamically typed at runtime; hints power linters, IDEs and type checkers — invaluable in bigger codebases.

questions.text: A context manager (usable in a with block) is any object that…
options: implements __enter__ and __exit__
inherits from contextlib.Manager
is registered in the interpreter's context table
defines open() and close() methods
questions.explanation: with calls __enter__ on entry and __exit__ on exit (even during exceptions) — files, locks and DB transactions all use it.

questions.text: asyncio helps most with workloads that are…
options: CPU-heavy, like batch image processing and heavyweight encryption
I/O-bound with many concurrent waits, like thousands of API calls
memory-bound, like giant in-memory sorts
single sequential file reads on local disk
questions.explanation: The event loop interleaves tasks while they await I/O on one thread; it does nothing for CPU-bound work (see: the GIL).

questions.text: is vs == : the correct usage is…
options: is for numbers, == for strings
they are interchangeable in modern Python
== compares values; is compares identity — reserve is for None checks
is compares the stored values; == compares the underlying memory addresses
questions.explanation: x is None is idiomatic because None is a singleton; using is for numbers/strings only works by accident of interning.

questions.text: pip freeze > requirements.txt captures…
options: only the packages your code actually imports
the packages' complete source code bundled together as a tarball
your project's entry points and scripts
every package in the current environment with exact versions
questions.explanation: freeze snapshots the whole environment — including transitive deps — which is why doing it from a clean venv matters.

questions.text: sys.exit(1) at the end of a failing script matters in automation because…
options: pipelines and shells read the exit code to decide success or failure
Python otherwise keeps the process alive forever
exit codes above 0 trigger automatic retries in bash
it flushes any remaining buffered stdout output, which a bare print does not
questions.explanation: CI jobs, cron, and shell && / || all branch on exit status — a script that fails but exits 0 lies to everything downstream.

questions.text: For CPU-bound parallelism in standard CPython, the usual tool is…
options: threading.Thread with a high thread count
the multiprocessing module (or ProcessPoolExecutor)
asyncio.gather over the heavy functions
inserting time.sleep(0) calls to yield between work chunks
questions.explanation: Separate processes sidestep the GIL, each with its own interpreter; threads and asyncio only interleave, they don't parallelise CPU work.

questions.text: Mutating a list while iterating over it (for x in items: items.remove(x))…
options: raises a SyntaxError before running
is perfectly safe in practice as long as the list stays reasonably small
skips elements unpredictably because indices shift underneath the loop
deadlocks the interpreter on the second pass
questions.explanation: The iterator tracks positions in the shifting list, silently skipping items — iterate over a copy or build a new filtered list.
